old_claim.note: Pastoralism provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No pastoralism prerequisite remains because Wadi Jilat 13 participated in already established steppe trading networks, and the graph's trade-route record predates the scoped pastoralist case.

source_shape.source_locator: Accepted manuscript p. 23 states that Wadi Jilat 13 was part of long-standing trading networks across steppe regions while identifying its inhabitants as the earliest unequivocal regional nomadic pastoralists.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The paper places the scoped pastoralists within pre-existing exchange networks rather than making pastoralism their prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pastoral communities can participate in and extend routes, but participation does not establish that the route concept causally depends on pastoralism.

ontology_before: A generic overview of later caravans made pastoralism an enabler of all trade routes, including an earlier route node.
ontology_after: Trade routes remain linked to exchange without a backwards dependency on the later source-pinned Wadi Jilat pastoralist case.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: trade_routes no longer directly depends on pastoralism.
would_reject_if: Direct evidence established that the specific trade-route scope in this graph began only through Wadi Jilat-style nomadic pastoralism.
short_reason: The scoped pastoralists joined long-standing networks and postdate the trade-route node.
